title: Chip Selloff Deepens as Nasdaq Books Fifth Down Day
content: US technology shares slid again on Friday as a renewed rout in semiconductors dragged the Nasdaq Composite to its fifth consecutive losing session, with the index easing about 0.2% to around 25,300 while the S&P 500 finished roughly flat near 7,354. Nvidia and Alphabet sat out a tentative megacap bounce as worries over the rising cost of AI infrastructure hit chipmakers, with Intel off about 3%, Marvell down 5%, Arm nearly 4% lower and Sandisk shedding around 10%.
summary: The Nasdaq fell for a fifth straight session as a fresh semiconductor selloff over AI infrastructure costs pulled Nvidia, Intel and Marvell lower.
context:
• The 2026 rally has leaned heavily on AI and semiconductor names, leaving the Nasdaq vulnerable whenever the trade wobbles.

• A brutal early-June session had already wiped out roughly $1tn in chip-sector value after disappointing guidance, leaving sentiment fragile.

• A hawkish Federal Reserve and a one-year high in the dollar have raised the discount rate applied to long-duration growth stocks.
happened:
• The Nasdaq Composite slipped about 0.2% to around 25,300, a fifth straight decline, as the S&P 500 ended roughly flat near 7,354.

• Nvidia and Alphabet failed to join a megacap rebound, while Intel fell about 3%, Marvell 5%, Arm nearly 4% and Sandisk around 10%.

• Investors rotated towards more defensive corners of the market as concerns mounted that soaring chip prices could squeeze technology margins.
implications:
• Continued doubts over AI monetisation and infrastructure costs could keep pressure on the most crowded megacap positions.

• A sustained de-rating of the Magnificent Seven would weigh on index-level returns given their outsized weighting.

• Any reassurance on AI demand or capital spending discipline could spark an equally sharp relief rally given stretched positioning.
importance:
• Semiconductors are the engine of the 2026 bull market, so their direction sets the tone for global equities.

• Narrow leadership means a handful of chip names can move the entire index, amplifying both gains and losses.

• The selloff is the clearest sign yet that investors are scrutinising the economics, not just the promise, of the AI build-out.

title: Volkswagen Plans Up to 100,000 Job Cuts, Four Plant Closures
content: Volkswagen shares fell about 3.9% after reports that Europe's largest carmaker is weighing one of the biggest restructurings in its near-90-year history, cutting as many as 100,000 jobs — roughly 15% of its workforce — and closing four German plants. The plan, due before the supervisory board on 9 July, dragged the DAX down about 0.6% to around 24,800 as the wider Stoxx 600 slipped 0.5%.
summary: Volkswagen shares dropped about 3.9% on reports it may cut up to 100,000 jobs and shut four German plants, weighing on the DAX.
context:
• German carmakers are battling slumping electric-vehicle demand, US tariffs and intensifying competition from Chinese rivals such as BYD, Chery and SAIC.

• Volkswagen is reportedly evaluating closures at Hanover, Zwickau, Emden and Audi's Neckarsulm site, putting more than 45,000 jobs at risk.

• Europe's industrial base has become a recurring drag on the region's equity indices even as luxury and defensive names hold up.
happened:
• Reports said Volkswagen could axe up to 100,000 jobs worldwide and close four German factories in its largest overhaul yet.

• Volkswagen shares fell about 3.9%, with Porsche, Zalando and Siemens Energy among the heaviest DAX decliners.

• The DAX dropped roughly 0.6% to around 24,800 and the Stoxx 600 fell about 0.5%, with carmakers and chip names leading losses.
implications:
• Union IG Metall has vowed to resist the plan, raising the prospect of industrial action ahead of the 9 July board meeting.

• Deeper cuts would underline the structural pressure on legacy European autos from cheaper, faster-moving Chinese EV makers.

• The restructuring is a test of whether cost reduction can restore margins without ceding further ground on volume.
importance:
• Volkswagen is a bellwether for German industry and a heavyweight in European equity indices.

• The scale of the proposed cuts signals how severe the competitive and demand shock to European autos has become.

• Job losses on this scale carry political and macro weight in the euro area's largest economy.

title: Asian Tech Splits: Nikkei Near Records, Hang Seng Slides
content: Asian equity markets diverged sharply over the week as Japan's Nikkei 225 pushed to fresh record territory on AI and semiconductor optimism before Friday's global chip selloff clipped the rally, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell 1.8%, or 405 points, to close at 22,672. The split underlined 2026's dominant theme: investors favouring AI hardware exposure in Japan, South Korea and Taiwan over Chinese consumer-internet platforms.
summary: The Nikkei flirted with records on AI optimism while the Hang Seng fell 1.8% to 22,672 on weak Chinese consumption and a tech selloff.
context:
• Japanese equities have been among 2026's strongest performers, powered by semiconductor and AI-infrastructure demand and a weak yen.

• Hong Kong and mainland Chinese shares have lagged amid soft consumption data and persistent caution towards platform companies.

• Regional sentiment has become tightly linked to the global semiconductor cycle, leaving exporters exposed to swings in US chip names.
happened:
• The Nikkei 225 hit a fresh record high earlier in the week before the worldwide chip selloff trimmed gains, with SoftBank sinking more than 5% on Friday.

• The Hang Seng dropped 1.8% to 22,672 as technology, retail and financial stocks led losses.

• Capital rotated towards semiconductor and AI plays in Tokyo, Seoul and Taipei and away from major Chinese platform companies.
implications:
• A deeper or more durable chip correction would hit export-heavy North Asian markets hardest given their AI leverage.

• Continued weakness in Chinese consumption keeps pressure on Beijing to deliver further stimulus to support equities.

• The Japan-versus-China divergence may persist as long as the AI-hardware trade outranks the consumer-recovery story.
importance:
• Asian indices are a real-time read on the global semiconductor cycle that drives world equity sentiment.

• The Nikkei's strength and Hang Seng's weakness capture the market's preference for AI infrastructure over consumer internet.

• Soft Chinese demand has implications well beyond equities, from commodities to global growth forecasts.

title: Bitcoin Slumps Below $60k to Lowest Since 2024
content: Bitcoin tumbled to its weakest level since September 2024, touching the high-$50,000s before steadying around $59,800, as a hawkish Federal Reserve and a one-year high in the dollar drained liquidity from speculative assets. Ether fell roughly 4% to about $1,556 and the total crypto market capitalisation slipped towards $2.14tn, with around $1bn of leveraged futures positions liquidated in the selloff.
summary: Bitcoin fell below $60,000 to its lowest since September 2024, with about $1bn in futures liquidated as the hawkish Fed and strong dollar hit risk assets.
context:
• Crypto has traded as a high-beta risk asset through 2026, amplifying both rallies and risk-off selloffs.

• The Fed rate cuts that bulls had positioned for are now largely off the table, with the dot plot pushing easing into 2027.

• A rising dollar and higher real yields lift the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding digital assets.
happened:
• Bitcoin touched its lowest level since September 2024, trading near $59,200 and down around 2.6% on the day before rebounding to roughly $59,800.

• Ether dropped about 3.8% to around $1,556, underperforming as risk-off rotation hit the sector.

• Roughly $1bn in leveraged futures positions were wiped out as the total market cap slid towards $2.14tn.
implications:
• A higher-for-longer Fed and firm dollar keep liquidity tight for the most speculative corners of the market.

• Heavy leverage leaves the market exposed to sharp moves in both directions on any shift in macro sentiment.

• Continued correlation with rates and the dollar means crypto is trading on macro headlines rather than its own fundamentals.
importance:
• Bitcoin increasingly trades as a barometer of global risk appetite rather than a hedge when the dollar is rallying.

• Large futures liquidations highlight how leverage can accelerate moves in the 24/7 crypto market.

• A breach of multi-year lows is a key technical and psychological marker for digital-asset positioning.

title: Brent Sinks to Four-Month Low as Hormuz Traffic Recovers
content: Brent crude fell to around $72 a barrel, its lowest since late February, and West Texas Intermediate slid to roughly $69 as tanker transits through the Strait of Hormuz accelerated following the US-Iran deal to end their war and reopen the waterway. The unwinding war premium has left WTI down more than 22% over the past four weeks, a dramatic reversal from the spring conflict spike.
summary: Brent dropped to about $72 and WTI near $69, four-month lows, as Hormuz tanker traffic recovered and the conflict premium drained away.
context:
• The Strait of Hormuz carries roughly a fifth of seaborne oil, and its disruption during the spring conflict had driven Brent above $120.

• A US-Iran memorandum ended the war, lifted the naval blockade and reopened the strait, allowing shipping to normalise.

• The collapse in the war premium has been one of the dominant macro forces of the quarter, easing headline inflation pressure.
happened:
• Brent fell to around $72 a barrel, its lowest since 27 February, while WTI dropped to roughly $69.

• Crude extended losses on negative carryover from the ceasefire as Hormuz transits accelerated.

• WTI has shed more than 22% over the past four weeks as the conflict risk premium has unwound.
implications:
• Cheaper crude eases imported inflation and gives energy-importing economies and currencies some relief.

• Sustained sub-$75 Brent would help cap headline inflation just as central banks weigh higher-for-longer policy.

• Any breakdown in the fragile US-Iran process would put the war premium straight back into crude.
importance:
• Oil is the key transmission channel from Middle East geopolitics into rates, FX and corporate earnings.

• A four-month low confirms that markets are pricing genuine de-escalation, not just a pause.

• Falling energy costs are a meaningful tailwind for disinflation and consumer-facing sectors.

title: Gold Firms Towards $4,090 as Equity Jitters Lift Haven Demand
content: Gold rose about 1.5% to around $4,087 an ounce as the renewed selloff in technology stocks revived safe-haven demand, though the metal remains well below its January record and roughly 5% lower year-to-date. Persistent central-bank buying continues to underpin prices even as a one-year high in the dollar and rising real yields cap the rebound.
summary: Gold climbed about 1.5% to roughly $4,087 an ounce on haven demand, though it stays below its January record and down on the year.
context:
• Gold surged to records in early 2025 on inflation and geopolitical uncertainty before retreating as the Iran conflict premium unwound.

• A stronger dollar and higher real yields raise the opportunity cost of holding the non-yielding metal.

• Record central-bank purchases and flat mine supply remain a structural support beneath the market.
happened:
• Spot gold rose about 1.5% to around $4,087 an ounce as equity-market jitters drove a haven bid.

• The metal nonetheless sits roughly 5% lower year-to-date and well below its January peak.

• A one-year high in the dollar limited the advance even as tech-stock weakness fuelled demand.
implications:
• Gold's path now hinges on the tug-of-war between haven demand and the drag from a strong dollar and high yields.

• Sustained central-bank accumulation could cushion any further downside even in a hawkish-Fed environment.

• A deeper equity correction would likely strengthen the safe-haven case and revive flows into the metal.
importance:
• Gold's behaviour is a key gauge of how investors are balancing inflation, real yields and risk aversion.

• The pullback from records shows how powerfully the easing geopolitical premium has reshaped commodity markets.

• Central-bank demand makes gold a barometer of reserve diversification away from the dollar.

title: Small Caps Lead as Investors Trim Megacap Tech
content: Small and mid-cap shares outperformed this week as investors trimmed crowded megacap technology positions and rotated towards cheaper, more domestically focused names, with bargain hunters also moving into healthcare and real-estate stocks. The Russell 2000 continues to trade at a steep valuation discount to the S&P 500, keeping the 2026 'great rotation' theme alive even as a hawkish Fed clouds the rate-sensitive trade.
summary: Small and mid-caps outperformed as investors rotated out of megacap tech, sustaining 2026's 'great rotation' despite a hawkish Fed.
context:
• Small caps spent years lagging megacap tech but have led in 2026 as breadth broadened beyond the Magnificent Seven.

• The Russell 2000 trades near a forward P/E around 18x versus roughly 26x for the S&P 500, a discount of more than 30%.

• Onshoring and fiscal support have been tailwinds for the domestically geared companies that dominate the small-cap index.
happened:
• Small and microcaps outperformed large caps over the week as money rotated out of repriced megacap tech.

• Healthcare and real-estate names attracted bargain hunters, while energy and commodity-linked stocks lagged.

• Financials, industrials and biotech featured among the stronger small-cap sectors.
implications:
• A hawkish Fed is a double-edged risk, as rate-sensitive small caps benefit less from the easing many had expected.

• Broadening market breadth would be healthier than leadership concentrated in a few megacap names.

• A sharp tech rebound could quickly pull flows back towards the largest growth stocks.
importance:
• The rotation is a test of whether 2026's market leadership can broaden beyond a handful of AI winners.

• Small-cap relative performance is a classic signal of shifting risk appetite and growth expectations.

• A durable rotation would reshape index positioning and benchmark-relative returns for active managers.

title: Dollar Hits One-Year High; Yen Near 40-Year Low
content: The dollar index climbed to about 101.80, its highest in more than a year, as a hawkish Federal Reserve under Chair Kevin Warsh widened the policy gap with other major central banks. The euro slipped to around $1.1342 while the yen languished near a 40-year low against the dollar at roughly 162, prompting Bank of America to trim its euro forecasts for the second half.
summary: The dollar index rose to about 101.80, a one-year high, as the euro fell near $1.13 and the yen sat near a 40-year low around 162.
context:
• The Fed's hawkish hold removed the easing bias and pushed any rate cuts into 2027, lifting the dollar's yield advantage.

• Easing Middle East tensions and resilient US data have reinforced the case for dollar strength into the second half.

• The Bank of Japan is tightening only gradually, leaving the yen exposed to wide rate differentials.
happened:
• The dollar index peaked at about 101.80, its strongest level since May 2025.

• EUR/USD edged down to around 1.1342 as the policy gap with the ECB widened.

• USD/JPY traded near 162, close to a 40-year low, with markets pricing at least one BoJ hike this year.
implications:
• A stronger dollar tightens global financial conditions and pressures emerging-market currencies and commodities.

• Persistent yen weakness raises the risk of intervention or a more forceful BoJ response.

• Dollar strength imports disinflation to the US while exporting tighter conditions abroad.
importance:
• The dollar is the key channel transmitting US monetary policy to the rest of the world.

• A one-year high reshapes the outlook for every other currency, commodity and cross-border flow.

• The yen's slide to four-decade lows is a focal point for intervention and carry-trade risk.

title: Treasury Yields Climb as Warsh Fed Bins Rate-Cut Bets
content: Treasury yields extended their climb as markets digested a hawkish turn at Chair Kevin Warsh's first meeting, with the two-year yield around 4.09% and the 10-year near 4.40% after a bear-flattening of the curve. The Fed held its policy rate at 3.50%–3.75% but stripped the easing bias from its statement and revised its dot plot to show no cuts in 2026, with the two-year having jumped about 16 basis points on the decision — its biggest Fed-day move since March 2008.
summary: Treasury yields rose, with the two-year near 4.09% and the 10-year around 4.40%, after the Warsh Fed removed its easing bias and ruled out 2026 cuts.
context:
• Kevin Warsh became Fed Chair in June 2026 and immediately signalled a firmer commitment to returning inflation to 2%.

• The June dot plot erased the previously pencilled-in cut for this year and lifted the median year-end funds projection to about 3.8%.

• Officials are weighing the durability of an inflation spike driven by the spring Iran conflict.
happened:
• The Fed held the funds target at 3.50%–3.75% and removed the easing bias from its policy statement.

• The two-year yield rose towards 4.09% and the 10-year to around 4.40% as the curve bear-flattened.

• The two-year had jumped roughly 16 basis points on the meeting day, the largest Fed-day move since March 2008.
implications:
• Higher front-end yields raise borrowing costs across mortgages, corporate credit and emerging-market debt.

• A flatter curve signals markets pricing tight policy now with slower growth later.

• Any sign that the inflation spike is fading could trigger a sharp rally as cut bets are rebuilt.
importance:
• Treasury yields are the global benchmark for pricing risk across every asset class.

• The removal of the easing bias marks a decisive shift away from the rate-cut consensus that opened the year.

• Warsh's hawkish debut resets expectations for the path of US policy into 2027.
